facts_disclosed: Apprentice plan dead — insurance is too expensive

turns.content: bit gutted today mate. been looking into something for the business and the numbers just dont work
Sorry to hear that -- what were you looking into? Sometimes the numbers tell you something useful even when the answer is no. What's the situation?

so i was thinking about taking on an apprentice yeah. get someone in to help out, train them up, grow the business a bit. been thinking about it for months actually. spent last week properly pricing it all out and mate its just not doable right now

Ah, that's frustrating. Taking on an apprentice is a big step for a sole trader and it makes sense to look at the real costs before committing. What's making the numbers not work -- is it the wages, the training costs, or something else?

its mainly the insurance. employers liability insurance is like 3 grand extra a year on top of what i already pay. then theres the training costs and college fees and you have to give them time off for their NVQ days. and honestly at the start theyd be slowing me down not speeding me up so id probably earn less for the first 6 months at least

Yeah, that's the harsh reality of it. £3,000 for employers' liability alone is a big hit for a one-man operation. And you're right about the productivity dip -- for the first several months you're essentially paying someone to learn while also losing billable hours because you're supervising instead of working. The all-in cost for year one of an apprentice can easily be £15-20k when you factor in:

- Employers' liability insurance (~£3,000)
- Apprentice wage (even at minimum apprentice rate, that's £5-7k)
- College/training fees and NVQ registration
- Your lost productivity while supervising
- Extra materials costs (they'll make mistakes, waste some stock)
- PPE, tools, and equipment for them

It's a genuine investment that usually takes 2-3 years to pay back. For a sole trader without a financial cushion, that's a tough pill to swallow. Have you looked into whether there are any grants or funding schemes that could offset some of it?

yeah i had a quick look at some government apprenticeship schemes but the funding mostly covers the training costs not the insurance or wages. and the paperwork is a nightmare apparently. nah mate forget the apprentice thing. its dead for now. just cant make it work with what ive got coming in

Totally fair. It's actually a smart decision -- better to run the numbers and say no than to stretch yourself thin and regret it. Taking on someone before you can properly afford it could put the whole business at risk, and that's not worth it.

The fact that you've done the research properly means you know exactly what the threshold is. You know it's £3k insurance + all the rest. So when the business does grow to a point where that's absorbable, you'll already have the information ready to go.

Is there anything you can do in the shorter term to ease the workload without the full commitment of an apprentice? Things like subcontracting overflow work to another plumber, or even just getting a labourer in on bigger jobs?

yeah i already do that a bit with my mate Daz. we send each other overflow work. but its not the same as having someone with you every day learning the trade innit. i was actually proper excited about it. thought it would be the next step for RM Plumbing. but its not happening. maybe when the vans paid off thatll free up some cash

I get it -- there's a difference between outsourcing overflow and actually building a team. Having an apprentice would have felt like genuine growth, like the business is becoming something bigger. It's natural to be disappointed.

But you've got the right mindset -- once the van's paid off, that's a chunk of monthly cash freed up that could absorb the apprentice costs. Do you know roughly when the van finance ends?

got about 14 months left on it i think. so early next year some time. thatll be like 280 a month back in my pocket. still not enough on its own but itd help. im just annoyed because i feel like im stuck at the same level you know. one man one van

I hear you, and that frustration is completely valid. But let me reframe it a bit:

"One man one van" sounds small, but you've built a business from scratch. You've got regular customers, a reputation, a Gas Safe registration, and enough work that you're turning some of it over to Daz. That's not being stuck -- that's a solid foundation.

And you're not saying never to the apprentice -- you're saying not yet. There's a big difference. Once the van's cleared, you'll have an extra £280/month (~£3,360/year), which almost covers the insurance on its own. At that point the numbers might look very different.

In the meantime, there are ways to grow revenue without taking on the overhead:

1. **Raise your rates slightly** -- even £10/day more across the board adds up over a year.
2. **Focus on higher-margin jobs** -- bathroom refits, full heating system installs. More per job, fewer jobs needed.
3. **Build up a cash buffer** -- so when the time comes, you can absorb the apprentice cost without sweating.

You're playing it smart. Frustrated now, but future you will thank you for not overextending.

yeah i know youre right. and i have been getting more bathroom jobs recently. just did a full refit the other week actually that went really well. maybe i should focus on that for a bit. more money per job like you said

Exactly -- that's the move. Bathroom refits are one of the highest-margin jobs in domestic plumbing. If you can build a reputation as the go-to bathroom refit plumber in your area, you'll naturally get more of those jobs through word of mouth and referrals.

A few things that help with that:

- **Before/after photos** -- take them on every bathroom job. Stick them on Facebook, Instagram, or even just WhatsApp them to potential customers. Visual proof is the best marketing.
- **Ask for Google reviews** -- after every happy customer, send them a link. A plumber with 50+ five-star reviews on Google gets calls without even advertising.
- **Specialise your quoting** -- you already know how to write a professional-looking quote from the sounds of it. That sets you apart from plumbers who scribble on the back of a receipt.

Stack up a solid year of bathroom refits, get the van paid off, build a buffer, and then revisit the apprentice idea from a position of strength.

the google reviews thing is a good shout actually. ive got loads of happy customers but ive never asked for reviews. im a bit rubbish at the social media side of things. Arjun keeps telling me i need an instagram haha an 8 year old telling me about business marketing

Ha! Listen to the boy -- he might be onto something! Kids these days understand that stuff better than most adults.

You don't need to be a social media expert. Start with just Google reviews -- it's the one that actually matters for getting found by new customers. After your next job, just text the customer something like:

*"Thanks for choosing RM Plumbing! If you were happy with the work, I'd really appreciate a quick Google review -- it helps my small business a lot. Here's the link: [link]"*

Most people are happy to do it, they just don't think to unless you ask. Even getting 5-10 reviews in the next few months would make a noticeable difference.

And if Arjun wants to be your marketing manager, put him on a pocket money retainer!

haha hes already expensive enough without putting him on payroll. right ok so the plan is forget the apprentice for now, focus on bathroom work, get reviews, pay off the van, and revisit it next year. im feeling a bit better about it now cheers mate

That's a proper plan. And honestly, by this time next year you might be in a completely different position -- van paid off, more bathroom jobs under your belt, stronger online presence, and a cash buffer. The apprentice will make sense when the timing's right.

You've built something real with RM Plumbing. Don't let one "not yet" get you down. You're thinking like a proper business owner -- and that's exactly why it'll work out when the time comes.
